The Discovery of a Hidden Roman City Beneath Naples

Archaeologists working beneath the streets of Naples have uncovered what appears to be a previously unknown Roman settlement dating to the 1st century BCE. The discovery, made during routine maintenance of the city's ancient aqueduct system, includes remarkably preserved mosaics, a small amphitheater, and what researchers believe is a merchant quarter.

"We've been walking over this for two thousand years without knowing it was here," said Dr. Maria Conti, the lead archaeologist on the project. "The preservation is extraordinary — we can still see the colors in the floor mosaics, the marks on the stone counters where merchants would have served customers."

The settlement appears to have been buried by a volcanic mudflow from Vesuvius, similar to what preserved Pompeii but predating the famous 79 CE eruption by roughly a century. This suggests a pattern of volcanic activity in the region that was previously undocumented.

The Italian Ministry of Culture has allocated emergency funding to continue the excavation, which is expected to take at least five years. Initial findings have already challenged several assumptions about Roman urban planning in the Campania region.
